sentenceFrame: The fish is on the ship.
celebration: FIRST PATTERN — the Pattern Tree is unveiled and the sh leaf goes up (leaf #1 of 32). Sheep joins the cast.

songs.title: Sh-Sh-Ship!
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
Sh! Sh! Sh-sh-sh!
(sh… sh… sh…)

[Hook]
Sh! Sh! Sh-sh-sh!
Two letters, ONE sound!
Sh-sh-ship! Sh-sh-ship!
It's a ship! (a ship!)

[Verse 2]
Sh! Sh! Sh-sh-sh!
What begins with SH?
Sh-sh-shop! Sh-sh-shop!
It's a shop! (a shop!)

[Verse 3]
Sh! Sh! Sh-sh-sh!
What begins with SH?
Sh-sh-shell! Sh-sh-shell!
It's a shell! (a shell!)

[Verse 4]
Sh! Sh! Sh-sh-sh!
What begins with SH?
Sh-sh-sheep! Sh-sh-sheep!
It's a sheep! (hello, Sheep!)

[Bridge]
A potato?! (NO!)
P-p-potato! Not SH!
(sh… sh… sh…)

[Outro]
Sh-sh-ship! Sh-sh-ship!
It's a ship! (a ship!)
Sh! Sh! Sh-sh-sh!
(shhh… quiet now…)
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: sh is the unvoiced hush /ʃ/ — the classroom 'quiet' sound — NEVER the letter names 'ess-aitch'. The '(sh… sh… sh…)' lines ride the hi-hats like steam. 'Two letters, ONE sound' is the week's whole lesson in one line. Verse 4 welcomes cast member Sheep. Outro dissolves into a real 'shhh' — the first sound song that ends in silence.

songs.title: The Fish on the Ship
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
(We can read. We can read!)

[Hook]
The fish is on the ship!
The fish is on the ship!
Where is the fish? (where? where?)
The fish is on the ship!

[Verse 2]
Sheep is on the ship!
Sheep is on the ship!
Wish, fish, wish! (wish! wish!)
The fish can wish!

[Verse 3]
The wish is big! (big! big!)
The fish is in! (splash!)
Where is the fish? (where? where?)
The fish is in! (swim, fish, swim!)

[Outro]
The ship… the fish… (shhh…)
A potato is on the ship!
(POTATO!)
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: Frame: 'The fish is on the ship.' — the first sentence built on a PATTERN. Verse 2 brings Sheep aboard and sets up the wish; verse 3 pays it off with the splash. Whisper the outro, then the potato hits loud. Prior-week frame reuse: the 'Where is the ___?' call-and-response carries over from Level 1.

spreads.artNote: A little wooden ship on a dark glassy sea, spotlight from above. New stage, same theatre — the children know this lighting from 26 weeks of books.
One bright fish mid-leap, caught in the spotlight. Full of intention.

spreads.text: The fish is on the ship!
spreads.artNote: The fish has landed flapping on the deck, wide-eyed. Comedy of errors begins — a fish OUT of water, on a ship ON the water.

CAST DEBUT: Sheep — a fluffy round white sheep with kind dark eyes — wearing a small blue sailor cap, calmly at the wheel. The children are taught the name by heart; 'Sheep' is not yet decodable (that day comes in Week 44).
The fish, eyes squeezed shut on the deck, wishing hard. One star out over the dark sea.
Sheep leaning in, cheering the fish on. The star brighter now.
The whole sky glows. The fish trembles with hope. Quiet spread — read it slow.
SPLASH — the fish back in the sea, joyous arc of water, Sheep waving from the rail. The wish came true.
The potato sits alone on the empty deck wearing a tiny paper sailor hat. No noun printed — the class SHOUTS it. Sheep stares at it. The fish peeks from the water.

sentences: The fish is on the ship.
extras: PATTERN TREE UNVEILING: the tree goes up on the wall — Level 1's letters are its roots and trunk. The class reads ship/shop/fish/wish aloud and the sh leaf (leaf #1) is hung. One leaf per week from now to graduation.
Sandpaper digraph sh: trace the two letters as ONE gesture, one sound — 'two letters holding hands'.
EAL minimal-pair sort: sh vs s — ship/sip, shell/sell, shack/sack (oral), sheep/seep (oral). Mandarin L1 children need explicit contrast here.
Moveable alphabet: build sh welded, then s-h-i-p, f-i-sh, w-i-sh, sh-o-p.

teacherFocus: THE FIRST PATTERN WEEK. sh is a brand-new sound made by two letters — different from ck (Week 21), which was two letters for a sound they already knew. Teach the sandpaper digraph as one gesture: 'two letters holding hands, one sound.' Unveil the Pattern Tree and hang leaf #1 with ceremony — this wall fills for the next 32 weeks. EAL priority: sh-vs-s minimal pairs (ship/sip) — Mandarin-L1 children merge these without explicit contrast. Welcome Sheep by name; the class will learn to SPELL that name in Week 44, and you may tell them so — it is a promise.
